concepts.lesson: A resource is anything that can be used to satisfy human needs and has value. For something to become a resource, it must be technologically accessible, economically feasible, and culturally acceptable.
concepts.real_example: Water in a river becomes a resource when it is used for drinking, irrigation, or electricity generation.
key_points: They must have utility and value
Economic and cultural factors influence resource use

concepts.lesson: Resources are classified into three main types: natural resources, human-made resources, and human resources. Natural resources come from nature, human-made resources are created by people, and human resources refer to people's skills and abilities.
concepts.real_example: Forests are natural resources, roads are human-made resources, and trained doctors represent human resources.
key_points: Human-made resources are created by people
Human resources represent skills and knowledge
All three types are important for development

concepts.lesson: Natural resources are materials and elements that occur in nature without human involvement. These include air, water, soil, forests, wildlife, and minerals. They are essential for survival and economic activities.
concepts.real_example: Minerals such as iron ore are extracted from the earth and used in industries.
key_points: Include water, air, minerals, and forests
Used for agriculture, industry, and energy

concepts.lesson: Natural resources can be classified as renewable and non-renewable. Renewable resources can be replenished naturally over time, while non-renewable resources take millions of years to form and cannot be replaced easily.
concepts.real_example: Solar energy is renewable, while coal and petroleum are non-renewable resources.
key_points: Examples include solar, wind, and water
Examples include coal, petroleum, and natural gas

concepts.lesson: Human-made resources are objects created by people using natural resources and technology. These resources help improve living conditions and support development.
concepts.real_example: Buildings, bridges, machines, and roads are human-made resources.
key_points: Created by humans using natural resources
Depend on technology and human skills

concepts.lesson: Human resources refer to the skills, knowledge, and abilities of people. Education and training help improve human resources and make them more productive.
concepts.real_example: Engineers, teachers, doctors, and scientists contribute to national development.

concepts.lesson: Resource conservation means using resources carefully and responsibly to prevent depletion. Sustainable use ensures that resources remain available for future generations.
concepts.real_example: Saving water and planting trees help conserve natural resources.

concepts.lesson: Sustainable development means meeting present needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. It balances economic growth, environmental protection, and social well-being.
concepts.real_example: Using solar energy instead of fossil fuels helps reduce environmental damage.

concepts.lesson: Resources are unevenly distributed across the world due to differences in climate, geography, and geological conditions. Some regions have abundant resources while others have limited resources.
concepts.real_example: Middle Eastern countries have large petroleum reserves, while other regions have fewer energy resources.

concepts.lesson: Responsible use of resources requires careful planning, efficient technology, and public awareness. Governments and communities must work together to ensure sustainable resource management.
concepts.real_example: Water harvesting systems help manage water resources efficiently in many Indian villages.
key_points: Planning is important for resource management
Responsible use protects resources for the future
